title: 6.1. KEY ABI

text: # 7. CLOB DEX Integration ## DEX (CLOB) SDK The Bitverse DEX (Central Limit Order Book) SDK is available in Go, Python, and TypeScript, providing third-party developers with one-stop on-chain trading integration. ### Features * **One-step calls**: Pass business parameters and a private key; the SDK automatically handles token acquisition, prepare, on-chain transaction signing, and broadcast * **Local private key signing**: Private keys are only used for local signing and never uploaded to the server; the server only receives the signed raw transaction * **Auto EIP-1559 support**: Automatically compatible with snake\_case gas fields returned by the server (e.g., `gas_limit`, `max_fee_per_gas`), supporting dynamic fee type=2 transactions * **Spot EIP-712 signing**: The SDK automatically completes EIP-712 order signing and on-chain transaction signing for spot orders * **Automatic token management**: Automatically requests and caches access tokens; supports custom token injection ### Supported Languages
title: 7. CLOB DEX Integration

text:
# 7. CLOB DEX Integration

| Language   | Package / Module           | Installation                        |
| ---------- | -------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| Go         | `bitverse-business-sdk-go` | `go get` / local module             |
| Python     | `bitverse-business-sdk`    | `pip install bitverse-business-sdk` |
| TypeScript | `bitverse-business-sdk`    | `npm install bitverse-business-sdk` |
title: 7. CLOB DEX Integration

text: # 7. CLOB DEX Integration ### API Endpoints * **Trading API**: `https://openapi.bitverse.zone/api/v1` * **Query API**: `https://api.bitverse.zone` ### Quick Start #### Go ```go import bitverse "bitverse-business-sdk-go" client := bitverse.NewClient(bitverse.Config{ AppID: "your_app_id", AppKey: "your_app_key", }) result, err := client.OpenOrder(ctx, bitverse.OpenOrderRequest{ TradingPair: "BTC-USD", Price: "65000", Amount: "0.1", Leverage: "1.1", Side: bitverse.SideBuy, OrderType: bitverse.OrderTypeLimit, }, privateKey) ``` #### Python ```python from bitverse_sdk import BitverseClient client = BitverseClient( app_id="your_app_id", app_key="your_app_key", ) result = client.open_order( private_key="0xyour_private_key", trading_pair="BTC-USDT", price="65000", amount="0.1", leverage="1.1", side=0, order_type=0, ) ``` #### TypeScript ```ts import { BitverseClient } from "bitverse-business-sdk";
title: 7. CLOB DEX Integration

text: # 7. CLOB DEX Integration const client = new BitverseClient({ appId: "your_app_id", appKey: "your_app_key", }); const result = await client.perp.open({ private_key: "0xyour_private_key", trading_pair: "BTC-USDT", price: "65000", amount: "0.1", leverage: "1.1", side: 0, order_type: 0, }); ``` ### Trading Flow #### Perpetual Contract Full Flow Recommended integration steps: 1. **Query perpetual markets** → Get `trading_pair`, `price_scale`, `amount_scale`, `min_leverage`, `max_leverage` 2. **Approve contract account** → ERC20 approve tokens before first use 3. **Deposit to contract account** → Transfer margin to the contract account 4. **Open perpetual order** → Submit an open order 5. **Query open orders / positions** → Get current orders and positions 6. **Close / Cancel perpetual orders** → Close positions or cancel active orders as needed 7. **Withdraw** → Transfer funds from contract account back to wallet #### Spot Full Flow Recommended integration steps:
title: 7. CLOB DEX Integration

text: # 7. CLOB DEX Integration 1. **Query spot markets** → Get `trading_pair`, `maker_id`, `base_token_contract`, `quote_token_contract`, `price_scale`, `amount_scale` 2. **Approve spot tokens** → ERC20 approve base and quote tokens before first order 3. **Place spot order** → Submit a spot order (SDK auto-handles EIP-712 signing) 4. **Query spot open orders** → Get current spot orders 5. **Cancel spot order** → Cancel active orders as needed ### Core Methods Overview #### Trading Methods (one-step, private key required)
title: 7. CLOB DEX Integration

text:
# 7. CLOB DEX Integration

| Feature         | Go                | Python              | TypeScript                |
| --------------- | ----------------- | ------------------- | ------------------------- |
| Perp Open       | `OpenOrder`       | `open_order`        | `client.perp.open`        |
| Perp Close      | `CloseOrder`      | `close_order`       | `client.perp.close`       |
| Perp Cancel     | `CancelOrder`     | `cancel_order`      | `client.perp.cancel`      |
| Account Approve | `AccountApprove`  | `approve_account`   | `client.account.approve`  |
| Deposit         | `Deposit`         | `deposit`           | `client.account.deposit`  |
| Withdraw        | `Withdraw`        | `withdraw`          | `client.account.withdraw` |
| Spot Approve    | `SpotApprove`     | `approve_spot`      | `client.spot.approve`     |

text: # 7. CLOB DEX Integration ### Precision Rules All prices and quantities are passed as string natural values, e.g., `"65000"`, `"0.1"`, `"1.23"`.
title: 7. CLOB DEX Integration

text:
# 7. CLOB DEX Integration

| Scenario                    | Precision Source                                      | Notes                                                                   |
| --------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Perp open price / quantity  | `list_perp_markets` → `price_scale` / `amount_scale`  | Decimal places cannot exceed the pair's precision                       |
| Perp close quantity         | Active position → `surplusQXV1`                       | Partial closing supported; recommended to use API return value directly |
| Spot order price / quantity | `list_spot_markets` → `price_scale` / `amount_scale`  | SDK internally scales before EIP-712 signing                            |
| Deposit / withdraw quantity | Server handles per token precision                    | Pass natural value, e.g., `"1.23"`                                      |
| Leverage                    | `list_perp_markets` → `min_leverage` / `max_leverage` | Only integers or 1 decimal place supported                              |

text: # 7. CLOB DEX Integration ### Response Format One-step trading methods typically return: ```json { "order_id": "backend-order-id", "prepared": { }, "signed_tx": "0x...", "broadcast": { } } ``` * `order_id`: Server-side order ID * `prepared`: Raw data returned by the prepare API (includes unsigned transaction) * `signed_tx`: Locally signed raw transaction from the SDK * `broadcast`: Broadcast result from `/orders/broadcast` ### Timeout Note Unsigned transactions prepared by the SDK are valid for **3 minutes** by default. If broadcasting fails due to timeout, simply re-call the corresponding one-step trading method. ### Error Handling The SDK throws exceptions when the API returns business or HTTP errors: * **Go**: Trading APIs return `*bitverse.Error`; query APIs return `*bitverse.QueryError` * **Python**: Raises `BitverseAPIError` * **TypeScript**: Throws an error object containing `status_code`, `code`, `error_code`, `message` ### Live Order Testing
title: 7. CLOB DEX Integration

text:
# 7. CLOB DEX Integration

Each SDK provides live order integration tests. Configure `app_id`, `app_key`, `private_key`, and other parameters before use:

* **Go**: `go test -run TestOpenOrderIntegration -v`
* **Python**: `python3 -m unittest tests.test_live_perp_order`
* **TypeScript**: `npm run test:real:perp-open`

text: # 7.1. DEX (CLOB) Go SDK Go SDK for Bitverse Business OpenAPI. Default domain for trading API: ``` https://openapi.bitverse.zone/api/v1 ``` Default domain for external query API: ``` https://api.bitverse.zone ``` The SDK automatically handles: * Applying for and caching access tokens * Calling the prepare interface to get unsigned transactions * Locally signing on-chain transactions using the provided private key * Calling `/orders/broadcast` to broadcast signed transactions * Automatically completing EIP-712 order signing for spot orders * Automatically carrying `TENANT-ID` / `CHAIN-ID` for query interfaces ### Installation Current local module: ```go import bitverse "bitverse-business-sdk-go" ``` Initialization: ```go client := bitverse.NewClient(bitverse.Config{ AppID: "your_app_id", AppKey: "your_app_key", }) ``` Initialization fields:
title: 7.1. DEX (CLOB)  Go SDK

text:
# 7.1. DEX (CLOB)  Go SDK

| Field          | Type       | Required | Description                                                                              |
| -------------- | ---------- | -------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `AppID`        | `string`   | Yes      | App ID for the integrator, used to automatically apply for access tokens                 |
| `AppKey`       | `string`   | Yes      | App secret key for the integrator, used to automatically apply for access tokens         |
| `BaseURL`      | `string`   | No       | SDK service address, default `https://openapi.bitverse.zone`, does not include `/api/v1` |
| `QueryBaseURL` | `string`   | No       | External query service address, default `https://api.bitverse.zone`                      |
| `TenantID`     | `string`   | No       | Query interface request header `TENANT-ID`, default `PHAROSMAINNET`                      |
| `ChainID`      | `string`   | No       | Query interface request header `CHAIN-ID`, default `1672`                                |
| `HTTPClient`   | `HTTPDoer` | No       | Custom HTTP client, default `http.DefaultClient`                                         |
title: 7.1. DEX (CLOB)  Go SDK

text: # 7.1. DEX (CLOB) Go SDK If you have already obtained a token yourself, you can call after initialization: ```go client.SetToken("your_access_token", expiresAt) ``` ### Overall Flow All trading methods in the SDK are one-step methods: the caller passes business parameters and a `privateKey`, and the SDK internally automatically applies for or reuses tokens, calls the prepare interface, signs the unsigned transaction, and broadcasts the signed transaction. One-step methods default to signing the unsigned transaction returned by the SDK service prepare as-is. The SDK automatically handles snake\_case gas fields returned by the server, such as `gas_limit`, `max_fee_per_gas`, `max_priority_fee_per_gas`, `chain_id`, and signs as an EIP-1559 type=2 transaction when dynamic fee fields are present. #### Perpetual Complete Flow Recommended integration order:

text: # 7.1. DEX (CLOB) Go SDK #### Timeout and Retry * On-chain transactions prepared internally by the SDK are valid for 3 minutes by default. * If broadcasting fails because more than 3 minutes have passed since transaction preparation, simply call the corresponding one-step trading method again. * If broadcasting succeeds but the cancel confirmation is not called, the server will perform background compensation; the one-step cancel method has already completed the broadcast of the cancel transaction. ### Methods Overview Trading methods complete `prepare transaction -> sign -> broadcast` internally within the SDK; pass the private key directly when calling: For a complete one-step trading demo, see: [examples/one\_step\_methods/main.go](file:///Users/shiyang/project-ali/bitverse-business-sdk-go/examples/one_step_methods/main.go).

| `SpotCancelOrder(ctx, req, privateKey)` | Cancel spot order                                                                   |

text: # 7.1. DEX (CLOB) Go SDK ### Precision Rules
title: 7.1. DEX (CLOB)  Go SDK

text:
# 7.1. DEX (CLOB)  Go SDK

| Scenario                    | Field                       | Parameter Rule                                                                   | Precision Source                                                                                          |
| --------------------------- | --------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Perpetual open              | `Price`                     | Pass natural value, e.g. `65000`, `65000.12`                                     | `price_scale` returned by `ListPerpMarkets`, decimal places cannot exceed this value                      |
| Perpetual open              | `Amount`                    | Pass natural value, e.g. `0.1`                                                   | `amount_scale` returned by `ListPerpMarkets`, decimal places cannot exceed this value                     |
| Perpetual open              | `Leverage`                  | Pass natural value, e.g. `1.1`, `10`                                             | Only supports integers or 1 decimal place, range see `min_leverage` / `max_leverage`                      |
| Perpetual close             | `Price`                     | Pass natural value                                                               | Handled according to perpetual price precision, it is recommended to query the corresponding market first |
| Perpetual close             | `Amount`                    | Pass `surplusQXV1` returned by active positions, supports partial close          | Use the value returned by the active positions interface                                                  |
| Contract deposit/withdrawal | `Amount`                    | Pass natural value, e.g. `1.23`                                                  | Currently supported tokens are scaled to 6 decimal places on-chain                                        |
| Spot order                  | `Price`                     | Pass natural value                                                               | `price_scale` returned by `ListSpotMarkets`                                                               |
| Spot order                  | `Amount`                    | Pass natural value                                                               | `amount_scale` returned by `ListSpotMarkets`                                                              |
| Spot EIP-712                | `ScaledPrice` / `ScaledQty` | SDK internally scales according to `price_scale` / `amount_scale` before signing | Caller usually does not need to handle manually                                                           |
title: 7.1. DEX (CLOB)  Go SDK

text: # 7.1. DEX (CLOB) Go SDK Notes: * When `chain` is not passed, the SDK defaults to `pharos`. * The `wallet_address` in one-step trading methods can be omitted; the SDK will derive it from the private key. * If `wallet_address` is passed, the SDK will verify that it matches the address derived from the private key. * The unsigned tx prepared internally by the SDK is valid for 3 minutes by default; after timeout, call the one-step trading method again. ### Response Structure One-step trading methods return `OrderResult`:

| `Signed`    | Local signing result, including raw signed tx and locally computed tx hash         |
| `Broadcast` | Broadcast interface return result                                                  |

| `Expiry`        | No       | Order expiration time; if not passed, `0`                                      |
| `PriceScale`    | No       | Price precision; if not passed, SDK fills from market info                     |
| `AmountScale`   | No       | Amount precision; if not passed, SDK fills from market info                    |
| `ChainID`       | No       | EIP-712 domain chain id, default `1672`                                        |
| `Contract`      | No       | EIP-712 verifying contract, default spot contract address                      |
title: 7.1. DEX (CLOB)  Go SDK

text: # 7.1. DEX (CLOB) Go SDK Spot order internal flow: 1. Query spot markets and fill `MarketID` / `PriceScale` / `AmountScale` 2. Scale `Price` and `Amount` according to precision 3. Sign the EIP-712 `Order`, get `order_id` and `signature` 4. Call `/spot/orders` to get on-chain unsigned tx 5. Sign the on-chain transaction and broadcast ### Spot Cancel ```go result, err := client.SpotCancelOrder(ctx, bitverse.CancelOrderRequest{ Chain: bitverse.DefaultChain, OrderID: "0xspot_order_hash", }, privateKey) ``` Fields are the same as `CancelOrderRequest`, where `OrderID` is the spot order hash. ### Query Interface The query interface defaults to requesting `https://api.bitverse.zone`, with default request headers: * `TENANT-ID: PHAROSMAINNET` * `CHAIN-ID: 1672` Common request `PageQueryRequest`:

| `Page`    | Yes      | Page number, starting from `0`                             |
| `Size`    | No       | Items per page; if not passed or `0`, SDK defaults to `10` |

text: # 7.2. DEX(CLOB) Python SDK Bitverse Business OpenAPI Python SDK. After passing in `app_id`, `app_key`, and wallet private key, the caller can complete the following in a single method call: 1. Apply for and cache `access_token` 2. Call the prepare interface to generate an unsigned transaction 3. Locally sign with the provided private key 4. Call `/api/v1/orders/broadcast` to broadcast the signed transaction The private key is only used locally for signing and is never submitted to the Bitverse server. The server only receives the signed raw transaction. ### Installation ```bash pip install bitverse-business-sdk ``` Local development: ```bash pip install -e . ``` ### Quick Start ```python from bitverse_sdk import BitverseClient client = BitverseClient( app_id="your_app_id", app_key="your_app_key", ) result = client.open_order( private_key="0xyour_private_key", trading_pair="BTC-USDT", price="65000", amount="0.1", leverage="1.1", side=0, order_type=0, client_order_id="A10001", ) print(result["broadcast"]) ```
title: 7.2. DEX(CLOB) Python SDK

text: # 7.2. DEX(CLOB) Python SDK ### Examples All one-step method demos are located in [examples/all\_one\_step\_methods\_demo.py](file:///Users/shiyang/project-ali/bitverse-business-sdk-py/examples/all_one_step_methods_demo.py). Usage: 1. Open the example file and replace the `APP_ID`, `APP_KEY`, `PRIVATE_KEY`, `WALLET_ADDRESS`, and trading parameters at the top 2. Change `ACTION` to the method name you want to execute, e.g., `open_order`, `spot_order`, `deposit` 3. Run: ```bash python3 examples/methods_demo.py ``` Supported `ACTION` values: ``` list_perp_markets list_spot_markets approve_account deposit withdraw open_order close_order cancel_order confirm_cancel_order approve_spot spot_order cancel_spot_order confirm_spot_cancel_order query_open_orders query_spot_open_orders query_positions query_perp_balance query_spot_balance ``` Note: Trading demos will actually sign and broadcast transactions; query demos do not use the private key. ### Overall Flow
title: 7.2. DEX(CLOB) Python SDK

text: # 7.2. DEX(CLOB) Python SDK All trading methods in the SDK are one-step methods: the caller passes in business parameters and `private_key`, and the SDK automatically applies for or reuses the token, calls the prepare interface, signs the unsigned transaction, and broadcasts the signed transaction. #### Perpetual Complete Flow Recommended integration order: 1. Query perpetual markets to get `trading_pair`, `price_scale`, `amount_scale`, `min_leverage`, `max_leverage` 2. Authorize the contract account before first use 3. Deposit to the contract account 4. Open a perpetual order 5. Query open orders or open positions 6. Close or cancel perpetual orders based on business needs 7. Withdraw if needed ```python perp_markets = client.list_perp_markets() perp_market = perp_markets["markets"][0] approve_result = client.approve_account( private_key="0x...", token_addresses=["0x97D41d7C18CADd7fdf26AdC9f5C5ab49FD6a7DEe"], ) deposit_result = client.deposit( private_key="0x...", token_address="0x97D41d7C18CADd7fdf26AdC9f5C5ab49FD6a7DEe", amount="1.23", )
title: 7.2. DEX(CLOB) Python SDK

text:
# 7.2. DEX(CLOB) Python SDK

open_result = client.open_order(
    private_key="0x...",
    trading_pair=perp_market["trading_pair"],
    price="65000",
    amount="0.1",
    leverage="1.1",
    side=0,
    order_type=0,
)

open_orders = client.query_open_orders(
    address="0xYourWalletAddress",
    page=0,
    size=10,
)

positions = client.query_positions(
    address="0xYourWalletAddress",
    page=0,
    size=10,
)

close_result = client.close_order(
    private_key="0x...",
    position_id="positionId-from-query_positions",
    price="65000",
    amount="surplusQXV1-from-query_positions",
)

cancel_result = client.cancel_order(
    private_key="0x...",
    order_id="orderId-from-query_open_orders",
)

withdraw_result = client.withdraw(
    private_key="0x...",
    token_address="0x97D41d7C18CADd7fdf26AdC9f5C5ab49FD6a7DEe",
    amount="0.5",
)
```

text: # 7.2. DEX(CLOB) Python SDK #### Spot Complete Flow Recommended integration order: 1. Query spot markets to get `trading_pair`, `maker_id`, `base_token_contract`, `quote_token_contract`, `price_scale`, `amount_scale` 2. Authorize the base token and quote token before first spot order 3. Place a spot order 4. Query spot open orders 5. Cancel spot orders based on business needs ```python import time spot_markets = client.list_spot_markets() spot_market = spot_markets["markets"][0] approve_result = client.approve_spot( private_key="0x...", token_addresses=[ spot_market["base_token_contract"], spot_market["quote_token_contract"], ], ) spot_order = client.spot_order( private_key="0x...", trading_pair=spot_market["trading_pair"], price="65000", amount="0.1", side=0, tif=0, salt=str(int(time.time() * 1000)), nonce=str(int(time.time() * 1000)), expiry="0", market_id=spot_market["maker_id"], price_scale=spot_market["price_scale"], amount_scale=spot_market["amount_scale"], )

| `quote_token_contract`   | Returned by `client.list_spot_markets()`, used for spot authorization                                                   |

text: # 7.2. DEX(CLOB) Python SDK #### Signature and Gas Field Compatibility One-step methods sign the unsigned transaction returned by the SDK prepare service as-is by default. The SDK automatically handles snake\_case gas fields that may be returned by the server, such as `gas_limit`, `max_fee_per_gas`, `max_priority_fee_per_gas`, `chain_id`, and converts them to field names required by `eth-account` signing. The current Python SDK does not actively refresh gas fees via RPC before signing; if the server returns dynamic fee fields, the SDK signs the EIP-1559 transaction according to the returned values. ### Global Conventions #### Default Environment
title: 7.2. DEX(CLOB) Python SDK

text:
# 7.2. DEX(CLOB) Python SDK

| Field           | Default Value                                | Meaning                                     |
| --------------- | -------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| `base_url`      | `https://openapi.bitverse.zone`              | Bitverse Business OpenAPI address           |
| `data_api_url`  | `https://api.bitverse.zone`                  | External query interface address            |
| `chain`         | `pharos`                                     | Currently supported chain identifier        |
| `chain_id`      | `1672`                                       | Pharos chain ID                             |
| `spot_contract` | `0x0ED7F1994907EC81E0522141cb33947A8E78E024` | `verifyingContract` for spot EIP-712 domain |
title: 7.2. DEX(CLOB) Python SDK

text: # 7.2. DEX(CLOB) Python SDK #### Price and Amount Precision All `price` and `amount` values in trading methods are passed as natural string values, e.g., `"65000"`, `"0.1"`, `"1.23"`.
title: 7.2. DEX(CLOB) Python SDK

text:
# 7.2. DEX(CLOB) Python SDK

| Scenario                       | Precision Source                                             | Description                                                                                                   |
| ------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Perpetual open order `price`   | `list_perp_markets().markets[].price_scale`                  | Decimal places cannot exceed the `price_scale` of the trading pair                                            |
| Perpetual open order `amount`  | `list_perp_markets().markets[].amount_scale`                 | Decimal places cannot exceed the `amount_scale` of the trading pair                                           |
| Perpetual close order `price`  | Price precision corresponding to open position/trading rules | Pass natural value                                                                                            |
| Perpetual close order `amount` | `surplusQXV1` returned by open position                      | Supports partial closing; it is recommended to directly use the value returned by the open position interface |
| Spot order `price`             | `list_spot_markets().markets[].price_scale`                  | The SDK scales up by `price_scale` before EIP-712 signing; still pass natural value to the server             |
| Spot order `amount`            | `list_spot_markets().markets[].amount_scale`                 | The SDK scales up by `amount_scale` before EIP-712 signing; still pass natural value to the server            |
| Deposit/Withdraw `amount`      | Server currently handles by token precision                  | Pass natural value, e.g., `"1.23"`                                                                            |
title: 7.2. DEX(CLOB) Python SDK

DEX(CLOB) Python SDK #### Common Return Values Normal high-level trading methods return: ```python { "order_id": "backend-order-id", "prepared": {...}, # prepare interface data "signed_tx": "0x...",# raw transaction signed locally by the SDK "broadcast": {...}, # broadcast interface data } ``` Authorization methods may broadcast multiple transactions and return: ```python { "prepared": {...}, "broadcasts": [{...}, {...}], } ``` #### Common Fields

| `chain`           | `str` | No       | Chain identifier, default `pharos`                                     |

| `amount_scale`    | Maximum allowed decimal places for amount |
| `support_trading` | Whether trading is supported              |

text: # 7.2. DEX(CLOB) Python SDK ### Perpetual Trading #### `open_order(...)` Open a perpetual order. The SDK calls `/orders/open`, signs the returned on-chain transaction, and broadcasts it. ```python client.open_order( private_key="0x...", trading_pair="BTC-USDT", price="65000", amount="0.1", leverage="1.1", side=0, order_type=0, client_order_id="A10001", ) ```

| `trading_pair`    | `str` | Yes      | Perpetual trading pair, must be from `list_perp_markets()`                                                                      |
| `price`           | `str` | Yes      | Natural price value; decimal places cannot exceed the `price_scale` of the trading pair                                         |

| `chain`           | `str` | No       | Chain identifier                                                                                                                |

text: # 7.2. DEX(CLOB) Python SDK ### Spot Trading #### `spot_order(...)` Place a spot order. The SDK first generates and signs an EIP-712 spot order, then calls `/spot/orders`, and finally signs the on-chain transaction and broadcasts it. ```python client.spot_order( private_key="0x...", trading_pair="PROS/USDC", price="1.23", amount="10", side=0, tif=0, ) ``` If you already have market precision information, you can pass it in directly to avoid extra market queries by the SDK: ```python client.spot_order( private_key="0x...", trading_pair="PROS/USDC", price="1.23", amount="10", side=0, tif=0, market_id="10001", price_scale=6, amount_scale=18, ) ```
title: 7.2. DEX(CLOB) Python SDK

text: # 7.2. DEX(CLOB) Python SDK ### Exception Handling The SDK raises `BitverseAPIError` when the interface returns business errors or HTTP errors. ```python from bitverse_sdk import BitverseAPIError try: client.open_order(...) except BitverseAPIError as exc: print(exc.status_code) print(exc.code) print(exc.error_code) print(exc.data) ``` ### Timeout Reminder The unsigned transaction returned by the server prepare interface is valid for 3 minutes by default. High-level methods sign and broadcast immediately; if you use the low-level approach to sign and broadcast yourself, ensure that `broadcast()` is called within the validity period. ### Live Contract Order Test The repository provides a live perpetual open order integration test: [tests/test\_live\_perp\_order.py](file:///Users/shiyang/project-ali/bitverse-business-sdk-py/tests/test_live_perp_order.py).
title: 7.2. DEX(CLOB) Python SDK

text:
# 7.2. DEX(CLOB) Python SDK

This test actually calls `client.open_order(...)`, completing token, prepare, local signing, and broadcast. Test parameters are written directly at the top of the test file and do not read environment variables.

Before running, edit the constants at the top of [tests/test\_live\_perp\_order.py](file:///Users/shiyang/project-ali/bitverse-business-sdk-py/tests/test_live_perp_order.py):

```python
RUN_LIVE_ORDER = True

APP_ID = "your_app_id"
APP_KEY = "your_app_key"
PRIVATE_KEY = "0xyour_private_key"

PERP_TRADING_PAIR = "BTC-USDT"
PERP_PRICE = "65000"
PERP_AMOUNT = "0.1"
PERP_LEVERAGE = "1.1"
PERP_SIDE = 0
PERP_ORDER_TYPE = 0
```

Then run:

```bash
python3 -m unittest tests.test_live_perp_order
```

Optional constants:

| `TIMEOUT`         | `20`                            | HTTP timeout in seconds                               |
| `WALLET_ADDRESS`  | `None`                          | Wallet address; `None` means derived from private key |
| `CLIENT_ORDER_ID` | `None`                          | Custom business order ID; `None` means auto-generated |
title: 7.2. DEX(CLOB) Python SDK

text:
# 7.2. DEX(CLOB) Python SDK

Price and amount are still passed as natural values; `PERP_PRICE` decimal places cannot exceed the `price_scale` of the trading pair, and `PERP_AMOUNT` decimal places cannot exceed the `amount_scale` of the trading pair. You can call `client.list_perp_markets()` first to check the precision of the target trading pair.

---
title: 7.2. DEX(CLOB) Python SDK

text: # 7.3. DEX (CLOB) TS SDK TypeScript SDK for Bitverse DEX (CLOB) — designed for third-party integrators. The SDK features one-step trading methods: simply pass a `private_key`, and the SDK automatically handles token acquisition, prepare, on-chain transaction signing, and broadcast. Integrators do not need to manually split the process into prepare / sign / broadcast steps. ### Installation ```bash npm install bitverse-business-sdk ``` ### Initialization ```ts import { BitverseClient } from "bitverse-business-sdk"; const client = new BitverseClient({ appId: "your_app_id", appKey: "your_app_key", baseUrl: "https://openapi.bitverse.zone", rpcUrl: "https://rpc.pharos.xyz", defaultChain: "pharos" }); ``` ### Overall Workflow All trading methods in the SDK are one-step: the caller passes business parameters and a `private_key`, and the SDK internally applies for or reuses a token, calls the prepare interface, signs the unsigned transaction, and broadcasts the signed transaction.
title: 7.3. DEX (CLOB) TS SDK

| `trading_pair`                  | `markets[].trading_pair` returned by `client.markets.listPerp()`                                                         |
| `price_scale`                   | `markets[].price_scale` returned by `client.markets.listPerp()`, used to validate the decimal places of the open price   |
| `amount_scale`                  | `markets[].amount_scale` returned by `client.markets.listPerp()`, used to validate the decimal places of the open amount |
| `min_leverage` / `max_leverage` | Returned by `client.markets.listPerp()`, used to validate leverage                                                       |
| Cancel `order_id`               | `orderId` of the order returned by `client.data.queryPerpOpenOrders()`                                                   |
| Close `position_id`             | `positionId` of the position returned by `client.data.queryActivityPositions()`                                          |
| Close `amount`                  | `surplusQXV1` of the position returned by `client.data.queryActivityPositions()`                                         |

# 7.3. DEX (CLOB) TS SDK

| Field              | Type         | Required | Description                                                                                                     |
| ------------------ | ------------ | -------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `appId`            | string       | No       | Integrator application ID. Required when `accessToken` is not provided                                          |
| `appKey`           | string       | No       | Integrator application key. Required when `accessToken` is not provided                                         |
| `accessToken`      | string       | No       | Pre-applied access token. The SDK will use it directly when provided                                            |
| `baseUrl`          | string       | No       | SDK service URL, default `https://openapi.bitverse.zone`                                                        |
| `dataBaseUrl`      | string       | No       | External query service URL, default `https://api.bitverse.zone`                                                 |
| `rpcUrl`           | string       | No       | RPC used to refresh on-chain gas fee before signing, default `https://rpc.pharos.xyz`                           |
| `defaultChain`     | string       | No       | Default chain, currently defaults to `pharos`                                                                   |
| `timeoutMs`        | number       | No       | Request timeout in milliseconds, default `10000`                                                                |
| `refreshGasFees`   | boolean      | No       | Whether to use RPC to refresh `maxFeePerGas` / `gasPrice` before signing on-chain transactions, default `false` |
| `gasFeeMultiplier` | number       | No       | Multiplier when refreshing gas fee, default `2`, used to avoid `effective gas price less than base fee`         |
| `fetchImpl`        | typeof fetch | No       | Custom fetch implementation                                                                                     |
| `headers`          | object       | No       | Additional request headers                                                                                      |
title: 7.3. DEX (CLOB) TS SDK

text: # 7.3. DEX (CLOB) TS SDK One-step methods sign the unsigned transaction returned by the SDK service prepare as-is by default. The SDK automatically handles snake\_case gas fields that the server may return, such as `gas_limit`, `max_fee_per_gas`, `max_priority_fee_per_gas`, `chain_id`, and signs as an EIP-1559 type=2 transaction when dynamic fee fields are present. If you want to refresh gas fees via RPC before signing, you can enable: ```ts const client = new BitverseClient({ appId: "your_app_id", appKey: "your_app_key", rpcUrl: "https://rpc.pharos.xyz", refreshGasFees: true }); ``` ### Precision Rules All prices and quantities are passed as string natural values, e.g. `"65000"`, `"0.1"`, `"1.23"`.
title: 7.3. DEX (CLOB) TS SDK

text:
# 7.3. DEX (CLOB) TS SDK

| Scenario                  | Field      | Precision Rule                                                                                                                                                                       |
| ------------------------- | ---------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Perpetual open            | `price`    | Decimal places cannot exceed the `price_scale` of the trading pair returned by `client.markets.listPerp()`                                                                           |
| Perpetual open            | `amount`   | Decimal places cannot exceed the `amount_scale` of the trading pair returned by `client.markets.listPerp()`                                                                          |
| Perpetual close           | `price`    | Pass the natural value price, it is recommended to control decimal places according to the corresponding perpetual trading pair `price_scale`                                        |
| Perpetual close           | `amount`   | Closing share, directly pass the `surplusQXV1` returned by the active position interface, partial closing is supported                                                               |
| Spot place order          | `price`    | Pass the natural value price, decimal places cannot exceed the spot market `price_scale`; the SDK will scale it by `price_scale` before EIP-712 signing                              |
| Spot place order          | `amount`   | Pass the natural value amount, decimal places cannot exceed the spot market `amount_scale`; the SDK will scale it by `amount_scale` before EIP-712 signing                           |
| Contract deposit/withdraw | `amount`   | Pass the natural value amount. The contract account tokens in the current document are processed by the server with 6 decimal precision, e.g. `"1.23"` will be on-chain as `1230000` |
| `leverage`                | `leverage` | Pass the natural value leverage, must be within the perpetual market `min_leverage` to `max_leverage` range, only supports integers or 1 decimal place                               |

title: 8. Team Introduction

text:
# 1.What Is a Decentralized Wallet?

In contrast to centralized wallets (exchanges) where the private key is in the hands of a third-party service provider, the private key of a decentralized wallet is kept by the user themselves and the assets are stored on the blockchain, the user is the real holder of the digital currency, the wallet is just a tool to help the user manage the assets on the chain and read the blockchain data, so there is no way to control, steal or transfer your assets. Therefore, decentralized wallets are difficult to be attacked by hackers centrally, and users do not have to worry about self-stealing or running away from the wallet service provider, because as long as you keep your private key safe when creating the wallet, your assets are still on the chain and can be displayed in a different wallet as well.

---
title: 1.What Is a Decentralized Wallet?

text:
# 2.What is MPC wallet?

Secure Multi-Party Computation, or MPC for short, is also known as SMPC. MPC ensures that you can use it while remaining invisible throughout the entire process, from private key generation to usage and storage. We utilize MPC to transform a single private key into distributed private key shards, effectively eliminating the risk of a single point of failure of a private key, while allowing multiple individuals on a team to co-manage assets. MPC works by splitting the traditional private keys into multiple pieces, distributing them in multiple places to ensure no one person has full access to the traditional private key. The major advantage here is that the private key is always used in a distributed manner. When a transaction signature is required, the parties involved in order to separately run a computation to make whatever you wanted to happen on the blockchain. The best part of this process is no single entity can ever get access to any private key: There is no single point of vulnerability. Even if an attacker tried to get access to one of the two shares, they can’t access all of the ‘secret shares’ simultaneously, making your digital assets much safer than in the traditional private key architecture.
title: 2.What is MPC wallet?

text:
# 3.How to prevent DApp Approve scams?

There are hidden dangers when a user needs to authorize the first interaction with a dApp. If the dApp is attacked later, it will be able to directly use its permissions to steal user assets. When a user conducts a transaction in a dApp contract, there will be an Approve button on the dApp page. The user must authorize to allow transactions, and this authorization means that the dApp contract has the right to transfer your assets. For the convenience of users, generally The default is to authorize an unlimited number of tokens. Once there is a loophole in the contract, or the contract administrator becomes greedy, all the tokens in the wallet authorized by the user will be transferred away. Bitverse reminds that users should not over-authorize when interacting with on-chain protocols, and at the same time, they should regularly deauthorize Dapps that are not commonly used, and pay attention to preventing fraudsters from “changing vests” to avoid asset loss. Therefore, you need to regularly clean up the permissions of dApps that are not commonly used or set an upper limit on the amount of token transfers.

---
title: 3.How to prevent DApp Approve scams?

text:
# 4.What Is the Miner Fee?

Miner fees (AKA network fee), commonly known as transfer fees as an incentive for miners to mine.Among EVM compatible chains, miner fees are determined by the Gas Price (unit price) and the Gas Limit (amount) consumed. The calculation formula is: Transfer fee/Gas fee/Miner fee = Gas Limit \* Gas PriceThe amount of Gas Limit is mainly affected by the complexity of the operation based on the content of the smart contract. The more complex the operation, the higher the Gas Limit.The Gas Price is set by the sender. The higher the Gas Price set by the sender, the faster the transaction initiated by the sender can be confirmed in the blockchain.Notes: The network fee for Ethereum is ETH; The network fee for BNBChain is BNB; The network fee for TRON is TRX; The network fee for Solana is SOL; The network fee for Polygon is MATIC; The network fee for OKExChain is OKT; The network fee for HECO is HT;Gas can be returned if it is not used up.The miner fee (network fee) charged by the transfer network will vary according to the network conditions. **Once the transfer is confirmed, the miner fee (network fee) cannot be refunded regardless of whether the transfer is successful or not.**

---
title: 4.What Is the Miner Fee?

text:
# 5.How does AI Agent Copy Trading work?

Users browse the **AI Agent Marketplace**, review each agent’s on-chain performance data, win rate, drawdown, and reasoning logs, then allocate funds to follow the agent. Profits are automatically split via smart contract (e.g. 10% to the creator). All trades remain in the user’s own **MPC wallet**; the AI never touches private keys.

---
source: 5.How does AI Agent Copy Trading work?
title: 5.How does AI Agent Copy Trading work?

text:
# 6.What is Leveraged Prediction Market?

It is Bitverse’s unique feature that turns prediction market events into **tradable leveraged contracts**. Users can open long/short positions with leverage on event outcomes or probability changes, just like regular perps, allowing stronger market expression and better risk management.

---
title: 6.What is Leveraged Prediction Market?

text:
# 7.How safe is my funds with AI Agents?

Funds stay in your own **non-custodial MPC wallet** at all times. AI agents can only execute trades within the limits you set (stop-loss, max exposure, slippage tolerance). The system has built-in circuit breakers and the AI can never withdraw or move your assets.

---
source: 7.How safe is my funds with AI Agents?
title: 7.How safe is my funds with AI Agents?

text:
# 8.What are 0 Fees and Pharos Network advantages?

Bitverse offers **zero trading fees** to remove friction and encourage frequent trading. **Pharos Network** provides deterministic execution, **0 Gas fees**, **0 Slippage**, and instant finality, delivering a smooth, low-cost experience across all assets and AI strategies.

---
source: 8.What are 0 Fees and Pharos Network advantages?

title: Agent Carnival

text:
Agent Carnival

loyment of intelligent agents at scale. ‍ To mark its 1st Anniversary, Pharos is partnering with Anvita Flow to launch the Skill-to-Agent Dual Cascade Hackathon - a 6-week builder event where developers create the foundational layer of the Pharos Al Agent ecosystem. ‍ The hackathon runs in two phases: first, developers build Skills - reusable functional modules that agents can call to complete tasks. Then, they combine those Skills to build fully functional Agents that can operate, transact, and interact on-chain. The best Skills and Agents compete for a 50,000 PROS prize pool, with top performers carrying their work forward into the wider Pharos Al Carnival. title: Agent Carnival

text:
Agent Carnival

submission by July 6) // Who Can Join Phase 1 Winners Prize Pool // Total Hackathon Prize Pool 50,000 PROS Phase 1 winners carry their prize eligibility forward into the Carnival. Pre-Season (Discord) 5,000 PROS 10 Winners from Discord Phase 1 - Skill Hackathon 20,000 PROS 40 Winners Phase 2 - Agent Arena 25,000 PROS Only Skill Hackathon Winner‘s Agent BUILD with us Create a Skill. Launch an Agent. Or do both. Skill A reusable module any Agent on Pharos can call — data fetching, content generation, on-chain actions, payments. Agent A full intelligent Agent composed from Skills, deployed on Pharos via Anvita Flow — ready to earn, transact, and serve users 24/7. Register to Build your Skill Join Discord Partners Host Sponsor Partners FAQ 1. Who can participate? Open to all developers globally. No restrictions on location or experience level. 2. Do I need Web3 experience? Not necessarily. Familiarity with APIs and basic development is enough to build a Skill. Web3 knowledge helps but is not required to get started. 3. What is a Skill vs. Agent? A Skill is a reusable functional module that performs a specific task (e.g. data fetch, content generation, on-chain action). An Agent is a co
title: Agent Carnival

text:
Agent Carnival

mplete intelligent assistant built on top of one or more Skills, deployed on Pharos via Anvita Flow. 4. Can I use an existing Skill in Phase 2? Yes — Phase 2 Agents must be built using Skills validated in Phase 1. You can use your own or other approved Skills from the Skill Hub. Only the Skill Phase winners can deploy their skills on Pharos, using Anvita Flow Infra. 5. What is Anvita Flow? Anvita Flow is a tool for developers to convert their Skills into Agent with one click. 6. How are prizes paid out in PROS? Prizes are distributed in PROS tokens to your registered wallet address after the winner announcement. Phase 1 winners are announced June 17–22, Phase 2 on July 24. 7. Where do I submit my work? All submissions are made via DoraHacks. Register there to get access to the submission portal and deadlines. Thank you! Your submission has been received! Oops! Community Center Your Gateway to Connect, Collaborate, and Set Sail with the Pharos Pharos Testnet Onboarding Guide May 16, 2025 ‍ What is Pharos Testnet? It s a super slick, high-performance EVM-compatible layer 1 built for RWAs. It’s a place where you can experience one of the fastest chains around—we re talking 30,000 TPS on Testnet. Pharos Timeline Empowers Structure Feature Prize Track Pharos Builder Base Camp Pharos Builder Base Camp: Bridging TradFi with DeFi to Unlock the Future of RWA, Payments, and Beyond. Build with us Bridging TradFi with DeFi to Unlock the Future of RWA, Payments, and Beyond The Pharos Builder Base Camp is a global initiative designed to empower developers, startups, and institutions to build the next generation of Real-World Asset (RWA) applications, decentralized payments, and financial innovations on Pharos Network—a modular and full-stack parallel Layer 1 blockchain. By leveraging the high-performance blockchain architecture, advanced modular stack, and cross-chain interoperability of Pharos, this program bridges the gap between Traditional Finance (TradFi) and Decentralized Finance (DeFi), unlocking new opportunities for financial inclusion, scalability, and market expansion. Participants will gain hands-on experience, expert mentorship, and access to Pharos’ ecosystem of SPNs (Special Processing Networks), native restaking, and high-speed parallel execution frameworks. Whether you re working on tokenized assets, decentralized payment rails, or cross-chain financial infrastructur
title: Pharos

text:
Pharos

es, the Builder Base Camp provides the ideal platform to turn ideas into reality. Builder Base Camp Timeline Registration Start 10th March 2025 Submission DDL 30th April Demo Day 15th May 2025 Registration Start 10th March 2025 Submission DDL 30th April Demo Day 15th May 2025 Event : Developer tour Station for each country - 2 in China, 2 in India, 1 in Vietnam (total 5) How Pharos Empowers Builders Pharos Network is uniquely designed to support complex financial applications and real-world assets with the following features: Modular Scalable Infrastructure L1-Base: High-performance data availability with hardware acceleration. L1-Core: A decentralized, high-throughput blockchain with subsecond finality for real-time transactions. L1-Extension: Enables Special Processing Networks (SPNs), cross-SPN interoperability, and native restaking to expand financial applications seamlessly. High-Speed Parallel Execution Data Processing Pharos Virtual Machine (PVM): Optimized for parallel transaction execution, reducing congestion and latency. Parallel Merklization Indexing: Enables high-speed asset transactions, real-time verification, and seamless integration with external TradFi systems. Fu
title: Pharos

text:
Pharos

ll Life Cycle Asynchronous Pipelining: Enhances efficiency by processing transactions independently, ensuring uninterrupted performance. Cross-Chain Interoperability Financial Ecosystem Integration Decentralized Data Exchange Protocol: Synchronizes external financial data sources with Pharos applications to enable real-world financial integrations. Adaptive Restaking Interaction Protocol: Facilitates shared security and liquidity circulation, allowing seamless DeFi TradFi asset movements (e.g., stBTC, stETH, tokenized securities). SPN-Native Restaking: Allows developers to create customized financial services leveraging the computing power of Pharos validators. Builder Base Camp Structure The Builder Base Camp Program aims to provide technical knowledge, mentorship and support to build and ship projects on Pharos. The program is divided into key phases, starting from onboarding and technical deep dives and going all the way to the pitching projects on the Demo Day. Each phase is designed to ensure that participants not only build but also deploy and scale their innovations on Pharos Network. Phase 1: Onboarding and Kick-Off The registration process for the Pharos Builder Base Camp
title: Pharos

text:
Pharos

Borrowers benefit from deep liquidity and capital-efficient borrowing options backed by a wide range of RWA and crypto collateral. Zona | Docs Supply Assets Supplying assets to Zona allows you to earn yield and boost your health factor. Supplied assets can be used as collateral for borrowing or simply to generate passive income. Step-by-Step: How to Supply 1 . Access the Zona app. Visit https://app.zona.finance/lend and connect your wallet. On this interface, you can see the total market size (e.g. total amount supplied and borrowed across all wallets), your personal details (e.g. your net deposits and yield, health factor, deposited/borrowed assets), individual market details, and a control panel to power your transactions. 2 . Select asset. Use the table on the left to select which asset you want to supply. Once you ve selected, click the arrow button on the right of the corresponding row to lock in the asset to your control panel. 3 . Input position size. You can type your desired supply amount manually or use our pre-set options to select 25%, 50%, 75% or 100% of your total wallet balance of that asset. 4 . Confirm parameters. Review the APY and health factor to make sure this is the transaction you want. Supplying assets will boost your health factor, making your position further away from liquidation. Re
title: Zona

text:
Zona

member, the lower your health factor is, the higher the risk of liquidation. 5 . Supply. Once you are comfortable with the terms, click the Supply button and confirm the transaction on your wallet. A pop-up will appear in the bottom-right corner of your screen to confirm that your transaction has been completed. Potential Risks Note that withdrawing will lower your health factor, making your position closer to liquidation. Be sure to monitor your health factor carefully! In extreme scenarios where the utilization for the asset you wish to withdraw is at 100%, you may be unable to withdraw immediately. Previous Lending Overview Next Borrow

text:
Zona

Zona | Docs Borrow Assets Borrowing on Zona allows users to access liquidity by using deposited assets as collateral. Loans are always overcollateralized, meaning the value of collateral must exceed the borrowed amount. Interest accrues on the borrowed amount and is paid to suppliers, with a small fee retained by Zona as protocol revenue. Preparing to Borrow Before borrowing, users must have supplied assets as collateral. The maximum borrowable amount depends on: The total value of supplied collateral The Loan-to-Value (LTV) ratio of each collateral asset (e.g., 70% for WPROS, 50% for certain RWAs or exotic tokens) The available liquidity in the asset being borrowed Current Health Factor (must remain 1 after borrowing) Step-by-Step: How to Borrow 1 . Access the Zona app. Visit https://app.zona.finance/lend and connect your wallet. On this interface, you can see the total market size (e.g. total amount supplied and borrowed across all wallets), your personal details (e.g. your net deposits and yield, health factor, deposited/borrowed assets), individual market details, and a control panel to power your transactions. 2 . Select asset. Use the table on the left to select which asset y
title: Zona

text:
Zona

ou want to borrow. Once you ve selected, click the arrow button on the right of the corresponding row to lock in the asset to your control panel. 3 . Input position size. You can type your desired borrow amount manually or use our pre-set options to select 25%, 50%, 75% or 100% of your total borrowable amount. 4 . Confirm parameters. Review the APY and health factor to make sure this is the transaction you want. Borrowing assets will drop your health factor, making your position closer to liquidation. Remember, the lower your health factor is, the higher the risk of liquidation. 5 . Borrow. Once you are comfortable with the terms, click the Borrow button and confirm the transaction on your wallet. A pop-up will appear in the bottom-right corner of your screen to confirm that your transaction has been completed. After Borrowing Monitor your Health Factor regularly, especially during volatile periods. Repay anytime by visiting the app, selecting the borrowed asset, and clicking Repay on the control panel. Potential Risks 1 . Liquidation Risk: If your Health Factor falls below 1 (e.g. collateral price drops or borrow rate spikes), liquidators can repay part of your debt and claim a di
title: Zona

Zona

scounted portion of your collateral. This may result in partial or full loss of collateral. 2 . Interest Rate Risk: Borrow rates are variable and increase with utilization. High demand can raise costs quickly. 3 . Asset-Specific Risks: Exotic collateral (e.g., certain RWAs) may have lower LTVs or Isolation Mode caps, limiting borrow power or increasing liquidation sensitivity. Always start with conservative amounts, monitor positions, and use tools like alerts or dashboards to stay safe. For more detailed coverage of risks, please refer to the Risks Mitigations section. text:
# Pharos Cargo Stylus

[![linux](https://github.com/OffchainLabs/cargo-stylus/actions/workflows/linux.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/OffchainLabs/cargo-stylus/actions/workflows/linux.yml) [![mac](https://github.com/OffchainLabs/cargo-stylus/actions/workflows/mac.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/OffchainLabs/cargo-stylus/actions/workflows/mac.yml) [![lint](https://github.com/OffchainLabs/cargo-stylus/actions/workflows/check.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/OffchainLabs/cargo-stylus/actions/workflows/check.yml)

A cargo subcommand for building, verifying, and deploying Arbitrum Stylus WASM contracts in Rust.

> [!NOTE]  
> Stylus contract verification will only be supported on Arbiscan for contracts deployed using cargo stylus `v0.5.0` or higher. We highly recommend deploying on Arbitrum Sepolia and verify your contracts on Sepolia Arbiscan first before going to mainnet.

## Quick Start

![Image](./header.png)

### Installing With Cargo

Install [Rust](https://www.rust-lang.org/tools/install), and then install the plugin using the Cargo tool:

```shell
cargo install --git https://github.com/PharosNetwork/pharos-cargo-stylus
```
title: PharosNetwork/pharos-cargo-stylus (GitHub)

text:
# PharosNetwork/pharos-cargo-stylus (GitHub)

Add the `wasm32-unknown-unknown` build target to your Rust compiler:

```shell
rustup target add wasm32-unknown-unknown
```

You should now have it available as a Cargo subcommand:

```shell
cargo stylus --help

Cargo command for developing Pharos Stylus projects
```

### Building the Project Locally

Install [Rust](https://www.rust-lang.org/tools/install)

Clone the latest [released version](https://github.com/OffchainLabs/cargo-stylus/releases) to your local device.

```shell
git clone --branch [VERSION_TAG] https://github.com/PharosNetwork/pharos-cargo-stylus
cd pharos-cargo-stylus
```

Run the following script to build and install the local binaries to cargo

```shell
cargo build -r
```

Add the `wasm32-unknown-unknown` build target to your Rust compiler:

```shell
rustup target add wasm32-unknown-unknown
```

### Overview

The cargo stylus command comes with useful commands such as `new`, `check` and `deploy`, and `export-abi` for developing and deploying Stylus contracts to Arbitrum chains. Here's a common workflow:

Start a new Stylus project with

```
cargo stylus new <YOUR_PROJECT_NAME>
```
title: PharosNetwork/pharos-cargo-stylus (GitHub)

text:
# PharosNetwork/pharos-cargo-stylus (GitHub)

The command above clones a local copy of the [stylus-hello-world](https://github.com/OffchainLabs/stylus-hello-world) starter project, which implements a Counter smart contract in Rust. See the [README](https://github.com/OffchainLabs/stylus-hello-world/blob/main/README.md) of stylus-hello-world for more details.

You can also use `cargo stylus new --minimal <YOUR_PROJECT_NAME>` to create a more barebones example with a Stylus entrypoint locally.

### Testnet Information

All testnet information, including faucets and RPC endpoints can be found [here](https://docs.arbitrum.io/stylus/reference/testnet-information).

### Developing With Stylus

Then, develop your Rust contract normally and take advantage of all the features the [stylus-sdk](https://github.com/OffchainLabs/stylus-sdk-rs) has to offer. To check whether or not your contract will successfully deploy and activate onchain, use the `cargo stylus check` subcommand:

```
cargo stylus check
```
title: PharosNetwork/pharos-cargo-stylus (GitHub)

text:
# PharosNetwork/pharos-cargo-stylus (GitHub)

This command will attempt to verify that your contract can be deployed and activated onchain without requiring a transaction by specifying a JSON-RPC endpoint. By default, it will use the public URL of the Stylus testnet as its endpoint. See [here](https://docs.arbitrum.io/stylus/reference/testnet-information) for available testnet RPC URLs. See `cargo stylus check --help` for more options.

If the command above fails, you'll see detailed information about why your WASM will be rejected:

```
Reading WASM file at bad-export.wat
Compressed WASM size: 55 B
Stylus checks failed: contract predeployment check failed when checking against
ARB_WASM_ADDRESS 0x0000…0071: (code: -32000, message: contract activation failed: failed to parse contract)

Caused by:
    binary exports reserved symbol stylus_ink_left

Location:
    prover/src/binary.rs:493:9, data: None)
```

To read more about what counts as valid vs. invalid user WASM contracts, see [VALID_WASM](./main/VALID_WASM.md).

If your contract succeeds, you'll see the following message:
title: PharosNetwork/pharos-cargo-stylus (GitHub)

text:
# PharosNetwork/pharos-cargo-stylus (GitHub)

```
Finished release [optimized] target(s) in 1.88s
Reading WASM file at hello-stylus/target/wasm32-unknown-unknown/release/hello-stylus.wasm
Compressed WASM size: 3 KB
Contract succeeded Stylus onchain activation checks with Stylus version: 1
```

Once you're ready to deploy your contract onchain, you can use the `cargo stylus deploy` subcommand as follows:

First, we can estimate the gas required to perform our deployment and activation with:

```
cargo stylus deploy \
  --private-key-path=<PRIVKEY_FILE_PATH> \
  --estimate-gas
```

and see:

```
Compressed WASM size: 3 KB
Deploying contract to address 0x457b1ba688e9854bdbed2f473f7510c476a3da09
Estimated gas: 12756792
```

Next, attempt an actual deployment. Two transactions will be sent onchain.

```
cargo stylus deploy \
  --private-key-path=<PRIVKEY_FILE_PATH>
```

and see:

```
Compressed WASM size: 3 KB
Deploying contract to address 0x457b1ba688e9854bdbed2f473f7510c476a3da09
Estimated gas: 12756792
Submitting tx...
Confirmed tx 0x42db…7311, gas used 11657164
Activating contract at address 0x457b1ba688e9854bdbed2f473f7510c476a3da09
Estimated gas: 14251759
Submitting tx...
Confirmed tx 0x0bdb…3307, gas used 14204908
```
title: PharosNetwork/pharos-cargo-stylus (GitHub)

text:
## Compiling and Checking Stylus Contracts

**cargo stylus check**

Instruments a Rust project using Stylus. This command runs compiled WASM code through Stylus instrumentation checks and reports any failures. It **verifies the contract can compile onchain** by making an eth_call to a Arbitrum chain RPC endpoint.

```
Usage: cargo stylus check [OPTIONS]
```

See `--help` for all available flags and default values.

## Deploying Stylus Contracts

**cargo stylus deploy**

Instruments a Rust project using Stylus and by outputting its brotli-compressed WASM code. Then, it submits **two transactions** by default: the first **deploys** the WASM contract code to an address and the second triggers an **activation onchain**. Developers can choose to split up the deploy and activate steps via this command as desired.

```
Usage: cargo stylus deploy [OPTIONS]
```

See `--help` for all available flags and default values.

## Verifying Stylus Contracts

See the formal Arbitrum docs on verifying Stylus contracts [here](https://docs.arbitrum.io/stylus/how-tos/verifying-contracts#reproducible-verification)

## Deploying Non-Rust WASM Projects
title: PharosNetwork/pharos-cargo-stylus (GitHub)

text:
# PharosNetwork/pharos-cargo-stylus (GitHub)

The Stylus tool can also be used to deploy non-Rust, WASM projects to Stylus by specifying the WASM file directly with the `--wasm-file` flag to any of the cargo stylus commands.

Even WebAssembly Text [(WAT)](https://www.webassemblyman.com/wat_webassembly_text_format.html) files are supported. This means projects that are just individual WASM files can be deployed onchain without needing to have been compiled by Rust. WASMs produced by other languages, such as C, can be used with the tool this way.

For example:

```js
(module
    (memory 0 0)
    (export "memory" (memory 0))
    (type $t0 (func (param i32) (result i32)))
    (func $add_one (export "add_one") (type $t0) (param $p0 i32) (result i32)
        local.get $p0
        i32.const 1
        i32.add)
    (func (export "user_entrypoint") (param $args_len i32) (result i32)
        (i32.const 0)
    ))
```

can be saved as `add.wat` and used as `cargo stylus check --wasm-file=add.wat` or `cargo stylus deploy --wasm-file=add.wat`.

## Exporting Solidity ABIs
title: PharosNetwork/pharos-cargo-stylus (GitHub)

text:
# PharosNetwork/pharos-cargo-stylus (GitHub)

Stylus Rust projects that use the [stylus-sdk](https://github.com/OffchainLabs/stylus-sdk-rs) have the option of exporting Solidity ABIs. The cargo stylus tool also makes this easy with the `export-abi` command:

```
cargo stylus export-abi
```

## Optimizing Binary Sizes

Brotli-compressed, Stylus contract WASM binaries must fit within the **24Kb** [code-size limit](https://ethereum.org/en/developers/tutorials/downsizing-contracts-to-fight-the-contract-size-limit/) of Ethereum smart contracts. By default, the `cargo stylus check` will attempt to compile a Rust contract into WASM with reasonable optimizations and verify its compressed size fits within the limit. However, there are additional options available in case a contract exceeds the 24Kb limit from using default settings. Deploying smaller binaries onchain is cheaper and better for the overall network, as deployed WASM contracts will exist on the Arbitrum chain's storage forever.
title: PharosNetwork/pharos-cargo-stylus (GitHub)

text:
# PharosNetwork/pharos-cargo-stylus (GitHub)

We recommend optimizing your Stylus contract's sizes to smaller sizes, but keep in mind the safety tradeoffs of using some of the more advanced optimizations. However, some small contracts when compiled to much smaller sizes can suffer performance penalties.

For a deep-dive into the different options for optimizing binary sizes using cargo stylus, see [OPTIMIZING_BINARIES.md](./main/OPTIMIZING_BINARIES.md).

## License

Cargo Stylus is distributed under the terms of both the MIT license and the Apache License (Version 2.0).
title: PharosNetwork/pharos-cargo-stylus (GitHub)

text:
# PharosNetwork/pharos-stylus-sdk-rs (GitHub)

The Stylus SDK

Rust contracts on Arbitrum »

## Overview The Stylus SDK enables smart contract developers to write programs for **Arbitrum chains** written in the [Rust](https://www.rust-lang.org/tools/install) programming language. Stylus programs are compiled to [WebAssembly](https://webassembly.org/) and can then be deployed on-chain to execute alongside Solidity smart contracts. Stylus programs are not only orders of magnitude cheaper and faster but also enable what was thought to be previously impossible for WebAssembly: **EVM-interoperability**.
title: PharosNetwork/pharos-stylus-sdk-rs (GitHub)

text:
# PharosNetwork/pharos-stylus-sdk-rs (GitHub)

For information about deploying Rust smart contracts, see the [Cargo Stylus CLI Tool](https://github.com/OffchainLabs/cargo-stylus). For more information about Stylus, see [Stylus: A Gentle Introduction](https://docs.arbitrum.io/stylus/gentle-introduction). For a simpler intro to Stylus Rust development, see the [Quick Start guide](https://docs.arbitrum.io/stylus/quickstart).

Comprehensive documentation on the Rust SDK can be found [here](https://docs.arbitrum.io/stylus/rust-sdk-guide).

This SDK has been audited in August 2024 at commit [#62bd831](https://github.com/OffchainLabs/stylus-sdk-rs/tree/62bd8318c7f3ab5be954cbc264f85bf2ba3f4b06) by OpenZepplin which can be viewed [here](https://blog.openzeppelin.com/stylus-rust-sdk-audit).

## Feature highlights

The SDK makes it easy to develop Ethereum ABI-equivalent Stylus contracts in Rust. It provides a full suite of types and shortcuts that abstract away the details of Ethereum's storage layout, making it easy to _just write Rust_. For an in depth exploration of the features, please see comprehensive [Feature Overview][overview].

Some of the features available in the SDK include:
title: PharosNetwork/pharos-stylus-sdk-rs (GitHub)
PharosNetwork/pharos-stylus-sdk-rs (GitHub)

text:
# PharosNetwork/pharos-stylus-sdk-rs (GitHub)

Additionally, the Stylus SDK supports `#[no_std]` for contracts that wish to opt out of the standard library. In fact, the entire SDK is available from `#[no_std]`, so no special feature flag is required. This can be helpful for reducing binary size, and may be preferable in pure-compute use cases like cryptography.

Most users will want to use the standard library, which is available since the Stylus VM supports `rustc`'s `wasm32-unknown-unknown` target triple. In the future we may add `wasm32-wasi` too, along with floating point and SIMD, which the Stylus VM does not yet support.

[overview]: https://docs.arbitrum.io/stylus/reference/rust-sdk-guide

## Don't know Rust?

The Stylus VM supports more than just Rust. In fact, any programming language that compiles down to WebAssembly could in principle be deployed to Stylus-enabled chains. The table below includes the official ports of the SDK, with more coming soon.
title: PharosNetwork/pharos-stylus-sdk-rs (GitHub)

# PharosNetwork/stylus-hello-world (GitHub)

```
git clone https://github.com/OffchainLabs/stylus-hello-world && cd stylus-hello-world
```

### Testnet Information

All testnet information, including faucets and RPC endpoints can be found [here](https://docs.arbitrum.io/stylus/reference/testnet-information).

### ABI Export

You can export the Solidity ABI for your program by using the `cargo stylus` tool as follows:

```bash
cargo stylus export-abi
```

which outputs:

```js
/**
 * This file was automatically generated by Stylus and represents a Rust program.
 * For more information, please see [The Stylus SDK](https://github.com/OffchainLabs/stylus-sdk-rs).
 */

interface Counter {
    function setNumber(uint256 new_number) external;

function increment() external;
}
```

Exporting ABIs uses a feature that is enabled by default in your Cargo.toml:

```toml
[features]
export-abi = ["stylus-sdk/export-abi"]
```

## Deploying
title: PharosNetwork/stylus-hello-world (GitHub)

text:
# PharosNetwork/stylus-hello-world (GitHub)

You can use the `cargo stylus` command to also deploy your program to the Stylus testnet. We can use the tool to first check
our program compiles to valid WASM for Stylus and will succeed a deployment onchain without transacting. By default, this will use the Stylus testnet public RPC endpoint. See here for [Stylus testnet information](https://docs.arbitrum.io/stylus/reference/testnet-information)

```bash
cargo stylus check
```

If successful, you should see:

```bash
Finished release [optimized] target(s) in 1.88s
Reading WASM file at stylus-hello-world/target/wasm32-unknown-unknown/release/stylus-hello-world.wasm
Compressed WASM size: 8.9 KB
Program succeeded Stylus onchain activation checks with Stylus version: 1
```

Next, we can estimate the gas costs to deploy and activate our program before we send our transaction. Check out the [cargo-stylus](https://github.com/OffchainLabs/cargo-stylus) README to see the different wallet options for this step:

```bash
cargo stylus deploy \
  --private-key-path=<PRIVKEY_FILE_PATH> \
  --estimate-gas
```

You will then see the estimated gas cost for deploying before transacting:
title: PharosNetwork/stylus-hello-world (GitHub)

text:
# PharosNetwork/stylus-hello-world (GitHub)

```bash
Deploying program to address e43a32b54e48c7ec0d3d9ed2d628783c23d65020
Estimated gas for deployment: 1874876
```

The above only estimates gas for the deployment tx by default. To estimate gas for activation, first deploy your program using `--mode=deploy-only`, and then run `cargo stylus deploy` with the `--estimate-gas` flag, `--mode=activate-only`, and specify `--activate-program-address`.

Here's how to deploy:

```bash
cargo stylus deploy \
  --private-key-path=<PRIVKEY_FILE_PATH>
```

The CLI will send 2 transactions to deploy and activate your program onchain.

```bash
Compressed WASM size: 8.9 KB
Deploying program to address 0x457b1ba688e9854bdbed2f473f7510c476a3da09
Estimated gas: 1973450
Submitting tx...
Confirmed tx 0x42db…7311, gas used 1973450
Activating program at address 0x457b1ba688e9854bdbed2f473f7510c476a3da09
Estimated gas: 14044638
Submitting tx...
Confirmed tx 0x0bdb…3307, gas used 14044638
```

Once both steps are successful, you can interact with your program as you would with any Ethereum smart contract.

## Calling Your Program
title: PharosNetwork/stylus-hello-world (GitHub)
PharosNetwork/stylus-hello-world (GitHub)

text:
# PharosNetwork/stylus-hello-world (GitHub)

```
RPC_URL=https://sepolia-rollup.arbitrum.io/rpc
STYLUS_CONTRACT_ADDRESS=<the onchain address of your deployed program>
PRIV_KEY_PATH=<the file path for your priv key to transact with>
```

Next, run:

```
cargo run --example counter --target=<YOUR_ARCHITECTURE>
```

Where you can find `YOUR_ARCHITECTURE` by running `rustc -vV | grep host`. For M1 Apple computers, for example, this is `aarch64-apple-darwin` and for most Linux x86 it is `x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu`

## Build Options

By default, the cargo stylus tool will build your project for WASM using sensible optimizations, but you can control how this gets compiled by seeing the full README for [cargo stylus](https://github.com/OffchainLabs/cargo-stylus). If you wish to optimize the size of your compiled WASM, see the different options available [here](https://github.com/OffchainLabs/cargo-stylus/blob/main/OPTIMIZING_BINARIES.md).

## Peeking Under the Hood
title: PharosNetwork/stylus-hello-world (GitHub)

text:
# PharosNetwork/stylus-hello-world (GitHub)

The [stylus-sdk](https://github.com/OffchainLabs/stylus-sdk-rs) contains many features for writing Stylus programs in Rust. It also provides helpful macros to make the experience for Solidity developers easier. These macros expand your code into pure Rust code that can then be compiled to WASM. If you want to see what the `stylus-hello-world` boilerplate expands into, you can use `cargo expand` to see the pure Rust code that will be deployed onchain.

First, run `cargo install cargo-expand` if you don't have the subcommand already, then:

```
cargo expand --all-features --release --target=<YOUR_ARCHITECTURE>
```

Where you can find `YOUR_ARCHITECTURE` by running `rustc -vV | grep host`. For M1 Apple computers, for example, this is `aarch64-apple-darwin`.

## License

This project is fully open source, including an Apache-2.0 or MIT license at your choosing under your own copyright.
title: PharosNetwork/stylus-hello-world (GitHub)

text:
# OpenZeppelin Contracts for Stylus

**A library for secure smart contract development** written in Rust for
[Arbitrum Stylus](https://docs.arbitrum.io/stylus/gentle-introduction).

## Features

- Security-first smart contracts, ported from the [`openzeppelin-contracts`]
  library.
- First-class `no_std` support.
- Solidity constructors powered by [`koba`].
- [Unit] and [integration] test affordances, used in our own tests.

[`openzeppelin-contracts`]: https://github.com/OpenZeppelin/openzeppelin-contracts

[`koba`]: https://github.com/OpenZeppelin/koba

[Unit]: https://github.com/OpenZeppelin/stylus-test-helpers

[integration]: ./lib/e2e/README.md

## Usage

You can import OpenZeppelin Contracts from crates.io by adding the following
line to your `Cargo.toml` (We recommend pinning to a specific version):

```toml
[dependencies]
openzeppelin-stylus = "0.1.1"
```

Optionally, you can specify a git dependency if you want to have the latest
changes from the `main` branch:

```toml
[dependencies]
openzeppelin-stylus = { git = "https://github.com/OpenZeppelin/rust-contracts-stylus" }
```
title: PharosNetwork/rust-contracts-stylus (GitHub)

text:
# PharosNetwork/rust-contracts-stylus (GitHub)

> [!NOTE]
> This library is designed to be `no_std`, which helps reduce wasm size. If you want your project to be `no_std` as well, ensure that your dependencies are not importing the standard library.
>You can achieve this by setting `default-features = false` for relevant dependencies in your `Cargo.toml`. For example:
>
> ```toml
> [dependencies]
> alloy-primitives = { version = "=0.7.6", default-features = false }
>
> ```
>
> You will also need to define your own panic handler for `cargo stylus check` to pass.
> Here's an example of a simple panic handler you can use in your `lib.rs` file:
>
> ```rust
> #[cfg(target_arch = "wasm32")]
> #[panic_handler]
> fn panic(_info: &core::panic::PanicInfo) -> ! {
>     loop {}
> }
> ```
>
> The library also works on an `std` environment, without the need to define a panic handler or making extra changes to your project.

Once defined as a dependency, use one of our pre-defined implementations by
importing them:

```rust
use stylus_sdk::prelude::*;
use openzeppelin_stylus::token::erc20::Erc20;

#[entrypoint]
#[storage]
struct Erc20Example {
    #[borrow]
    pub erc20: Erc20,
}

#[public]
#[inherit(Erc20)]
impl Erc20Example {}
```
title: PharosNetwork/rust-contracts-stylus (GitHub)
